channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran’s Loyalists Promote a Wider Nationalism, Unveiled Women Included
item.description: Government supporters are showing off new ties with alleged former dissidents in a bid to show that they can withstand enemies at home as well as abroad.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran last month where some of the women were partially veiled and some dressed more conservatively.

item.title: Myanmar’s Civil War Has Become an Apocalypse
item.description: Overshadowed by conflicts in Ukraine, Gaza and Lebanon, the fighting in Myanmar’s isolated heartland has reached new depths.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Daniel Berehulak
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: On a forgotten frontline, the rebels fight deep in the jungle.

item.title: See Record-Breaking Extreme Heat Wave Across Europe in Photos
item.description: Temperature records were broken in several European countries on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: The banks of the Canal Saint-Martin became a makeshift beach in Paris as city dwellers sought cool air.

item.title: Rubio Seeks to Reassure Gulf Arab Partners on Iran Deal
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio was traveling among the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait and Bahrain, which were attacked by Iran during the war.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley, Edward Wong, Euan Ward and John Yoon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio greeting the Emirati ambassador to the U.S., Yousef al-Otaiba, in Abu Dhabi, U.A.E., on Tuesday.

item.title: France Identifies Its First Case of Ebola
item.description: A doctor who had traveled to the Democratic Republic of Congo was infected, the French health ministry said. The authorities said the risk to the wider population was low.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers last month in the Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of an Ebola outbreak that has killed more than 200 people.

item.title: ICC Judges Sue Trump Administration Over Sanctions
item.description: Kimberly Prost, a Canadian judge at the International Criminal Court, and two colleagues are suing the administration, saying the sanctions were a form of retaliation.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Amanda Taub
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: Kimberly Prost, a Canadian judge at the International Criminal Court, along with two others, is suing President Trump and his administration, claiming the penalties exceeded his authority.

item.title: Extreme Heat Across Europe Creates Divide Over Leaving Schools Open
item.description: Few schools in Britain or France have air-conditioning to keep children cool in soaring temperatures, leaving parents, teachers and officials divided about what to do.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Students eating ice pops during class at an elementary school in Grays, England, on Wednesday.

item.title: Israel Says It Has Trapped Hezbollah Fighters Underground in Southern Lebanon
item.description: The Israeli military says thousands of troops have surrounded a large tunnel network in southern Lebanon. The standoff is at the center of intense clashes in the area recently.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger, Christina Goldbaum, Euan Ward and Samuel Granados

item.title: Cuba Announces Sweeping Overhaul of Struggling Communist Economy, But Obstacles Remain
item.description: The Cuban government announced a major economic restructuring, but experts say most changes would depend on buy-in from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Eliana Aponte for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers in the production workshop of Dforja Creations, a private furniture company in Havana, in 2024.

item.title: Bahrain Restricts Shiite Holy Day Amid Iran War Crackdown
item.description: The restrictions are the latest in a string of measures Bahrain has taken against Shiite citizens in the Gulf country, with many accused of having loyalties to Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Observing Ashura in the village of Sanabis, west of Manama, Bahrain, in 2015.

item.title: The Wife of Spain’s Leader Is Accused of Graft. It May Actually Help Him.
item.description: People close to Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez keep being investigated for corruption. Analysts say weaknesses in the latest case could help him discredit the others.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:description: Spain’s prime minister, Pedro Sánchez, and his wife, Begoña Gómez, in Barcelona this month.

item.title: Could Brazil’s Next Big Soccer Star Be Scouted By AI?
item.description: Recruitment apps powered by artificial intelligence are gaining ground in Brazil, promising to even the playing field and find more soccer talent.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Victor Moriyama

item.title: With a Turkish ‘Abrazo,’ Istanbul Ascends as a Tango Capital
item.description: Istanbul’s many tango schools, clubs and skilled dancers have won the city recognition, even among Argentine maestros, as a global tango destination.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard, Safak Timur and Laura Boushnak

item.title: The Ukraine War Amputees Embracing Wake Boarding and Jujitsu
item.description: As the number of amputees in Ukraine soars, many are bonding by learning new sports, challenging both their bodies and their ideas of what they can do.
item.dc:creator: Siobhán O’Grady, Liubov Sholudko, Yurii Shyvala and Oksana Parafeniuk
item.media:credit: Oksana Parafeniuk for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ukrainian war veteran Volodymyr Kuzmenko tries wakeboarding for the first time in Kyiv, in May.

item.title: Venezuela’s Neighbors Offer Aid in Earthquake Response
item.description: Officials from the United States, El Salvador and the Dominican Republic said they would send rescue teams and humanitarian assistance to Caracas.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency services searched the site of a collapsed building after an earthquake struck Caracas on Wednesday.

item.title: To Escape the Heat, Parisians Jump into an Old Industrial Canal
item.description: Crowds are filling the Canal Saint-Martin in Paris, as temperatures soar above 100 degrees Fahrenheit. Forty people drowned while swimming in other waterways.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Swimming in Canal St. Martin in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: A Puppy-Abuse Case in China, Caught on Video, Prompts Rare Public Protest
item.description: After a man was filmed torturing dogs, hundreds gathered in a rare demonstration that the police tried to break up. For many, the sit-in was their first civic action.
item.dc:creator: Murphy Zhao and Lily Kuo
item.media:credit: Video obtained by The New York Times

item.title: 7.5-Magnitude Quake Is Largest to Hit Venezuela in More Than a Century
item.description: The U.S. Geological Survey estimated that it could be far deadlier than a 7.7-magnitude earthquake that struck the country in 1900.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff

item.title: Photos: Earthquakes Strike Venezuela
item.description: After two powerful quakes shook the South American country, concern is growing that the damage could be devastating.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emergency workers attempting to rescue people trapped in the rubble of a residential building that collapsed during an earthquake in Caracas, Venezuela, on Wednesday.

item.title: Venezuela Hit by 7.1-Magnitude Earthquake
item.dc:creator: María Victoria Fermín and Simon Romero

item.title: A Climate Chaos Maker
item.description: Global warming is causing extreme heat around the world. Now El Niño, a natural phenomenon that disrupts global weather patterns, is also at work.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Joann Pai for The New York Times
item.media:description: Outside the Louvre museum in Paris, yesterday.

item.title: Why Is Europe the Fastest-Warming Continent
item.description: The burning of fossil fuels is raising temperatures worldwide, but local factors, on land and at sea, determine which regions warm most rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: Morning commuters on the London Bridge on Wednesday, where temperatures reached 96 degrees Fahrenheit, the hottest recorded June day in the United Kingdom.

item.title: China Detained 2 Japanese on Suspicion of Smuggling
item.description: The Japanese nationals were detained in May in the northeastern Chinese city of Dalian, Japan’s government said, amid tensions between the two countries.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Chinese security at the entrance to the Japanese Embassy in Beijing last year. Tensions between the two countries have been rising.

item.title: What to Know About Myanmar’s Chaotic War
item.description: The Southeast Asian nation recently experienced its highest monthly civilian death toll since the war began in 2021.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:description: A rebel soldier at a frontline position in the Anyar region of Myanmar.

item.title: On the Front Lines of Myanmar’s Forgotten War
item.description: Our Times correspondent, Hannah Beech, travels to the center of the armed resistance movement in Myanmar and meets rebel fighters who are outgunned and undermanned after years of fighting in a civil war.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech, Daniel Berehulak, Nailah Morgan and Jiwoong Hong

item.title: Is the Australian Prime Minister’s Pragmatism Enough in Uncertain Times?
item.description: Buffeted by global turmoil and a populist far-right ascendance at home, Anthony Albanese is trying to keep the country feeling hopeful.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:credit: Adam Ferguson for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s Colombia Prize
item.description: Abelardo De La Espriella appears set to be the country’s next president. It adds another right-wing populist leader to a growing roster around the world.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: De La Espriella’s supporters in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday.

item.title: Ukrainian Women Plead for News of Disabled Relatives Held by Russia
item.description: Four years after Ukraine was invaded, many of the people who lived in institutional settings remain unaccounted for.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Oksana Oliinyk, Hanna Zamyshliaieva and Larysa Branytska at a news conference on Tuesday in Kyiv.

item.title: U.N. Report Says Israeli Killings of Gaza Children Post-Truce Amount to Genocide
item.description: Israel’s U.N. mission dismissed the report as a “libelous sham,” and called the investigating commission a “fundamentally flawed mechanism.”
item.dc:creator: Nick Cumming-Bruce
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: A father carried the body of his 3‑year‑old, who was killed in an Israeli strike, according to medics, during his funeral in Gaza City in April.

item.title: In Middle East, Rubio Says ‘No Country’ Can Charge for Hormuz Traffic
item.description: The secretary of state dismissed Iran’s talk of charging ships to pass through the vital Persian Gulf portal as prohibited under international law.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio spoke to reporters shortly after arriving in Abu Dhabi on Tuesday.

item.title: U.N. Sets Plan to Evacuate Stranded Ships Out of Persian Gulf
item.description: The International Maritime Organization, working with multiple countries, said it has ensured that vessels can sail safely through the Strait of Hormuz after months of being idled.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Hundreds of ships have been stranded in the Persian Gulf since the war began, waiting for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Artist Pulls Work From London Museum After Clash Over Churchill’s Legacy
item.description: A historian and others said that a video installation had incorrectly blamed Winston Churchill for a famine in colonial India.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Portrait Gallery in London. A video installation called “Persistence” was taken down this week after a historian criticized a reference to Winston Churchill.

item.title: U.S. Warns of ‘Imminent’ Atrocities in El Obeid, Sudan
item.description: A highway running through El Obeid links Darfur in west Sudan to the east, making it a strategically significant battleground.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Brian O. Otieno
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:description: A camp for displaced persons in El Obeid, Sudan, in January. The crisis has forced around 14 million people from their homes.

item.title: Britain Is Still Deep in the Shadow of Brexit
item.description: Ten years after a slim majority voted to leave the European Union, the economic and political effects of that decision continue to disrupt the United Kingdom.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Adam Ferguson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A “Vote Leave” rally in London in June 2016. The proposal to separate from the European Union narrowly passed.

item.title: Israeli Soldiers Fatally Shoot 2 People in Southern Lebanon, Health Officials Say
item.description: The shooting came a day after Israeli troops began operating under new orders intended to reduce the risk of flare-ups in its conflict with Iran-backed Hezbollah militants.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Damaged buildings in Nabatieh, Lebanon, on Sunday. Israeli soldiers shot and killed two people near the city on Tuesday, Lebanese health officials said.

item.title: The Iran War’s Persistent Threat to Farmers in Poor Countries
item.description: A reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would do little to swiftly ease the pain inflicted by higher prices for fertilizer, food and fuel in Ivory Coast.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman and Finbarr O’Reilly
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: U.S. and Iran’s Conflicting Claims on Nuclear Inspection Show Challenge Ahead
item.description: President Trump said that in the latest round of peace talks, Iran had agreed to full inspection of its nuclear sites. Iran’s government said it had not.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: A satellite imagery taken on June 21, 2025, shows destroyed buildings at Isfahan nuclear site, Iran.

item.title: After Wars and False Starts, Cautious Optimism for U.S.-Iran Talks
item.description: Tehran and Washington face myriad negotiating pitfalls. But both sides are exhausted and running out of options. They may finally need a deal.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In Tehran this month. Political analysts say both Iran and the United States now have a real need for progress.

item.title: The Extraordinary Embezzlement Case That Rocked Scottish Politics
item.description: Peter Murrell, the husband of the former Scottish leader Nicola Sturgeon, was sentenced on Tuesday after he admitted to buying a bizarre range of items with the Scottish National Party’s money.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Jane Barlow/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Peter Murrell, center, arriving at Edinburgh High Court in May. He was sentenced on Tuesday to more than five years in prison.

item.title: 40 People Drown in France Amid Scorching Temperatures
item.description: Most of the victims were young people swimming in unsupervised areas, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: People trying to find respite in the shade in Toulouse, France, on Monday.

item.title: Japan to Raise Some Tourist Visa Fees More Than 400 Percent
item.description: The hike, from around $18 to $93 for a single trip, will apply to visitors from countries including China, India and Vietnam, but not most Western travelers.
item.dc:creator: Hisako Ueno and Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: Kentaro Takahashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tourists at the starting point to climb Mount Fuji in Japan, earlier this year.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes, Leo Sands and Nick Cumming-Bruce

item.title: ‘A Terrible Inheritance’: Could Andy Burnham Succeed Where Starmer Failed?
item.description: The likely successor to Prime Minister Keir Starmer will inherit the same challenges of economic stagnation and ascendant populism. Will a divided nation be prepared to give him time?
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham celebrating after his swearing-in in Parliament on Monday.

item.title: Soldier, Scholar and Symbol: Marc Bloch Is Elevated to France’s Panthéon
item.description: French politicians, including those on the far right, are embracing a Jewish Resistance fighter murdered by the Gestapo in 1944.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Portraits of Marc Bloch and his wife, Simonne Vidal, in Paris on Monday. Their caskets are set to be installed in France’s Panthéon on Tuesday.

item.title: China Takes Supercomputer Crown From U.S. For First Time Since 2017
item.description: A supercomputer in Shenzhen was declared the world’s fastest. It uses only standard microprocessors and not the special-purpose chips called graphics processing units.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark
item.media:description: China’s National Supercomputing Center in Wuxi in 2020. LineShine, a supercomputer in Shenzhen, has been declared the world’s fastest.

item.title: How Mark Rutte of NATO Manages an Unpredictable Trump
item.description: Secretary General Mark Rutte is headed to Washington. His style has at times frustrated the very European leaders who need him to hold the alliance together.
item.dc:creator: Mara Hvistendahl and Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump and Mark Rutte, the NATO secretary general, at the World Economic Forum in January.

item.title: Iranian Singer Sentenced to 74 Lashes for Performing Without Hijab
item.description: Parastoo Ahmadi and her band will also be barred from leaving the country or performing for two years, making clear to Iranians that their new leaders may be just as severe as those killed in the war.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin and Shirin Hakim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Iran Makes Moves to Assert Control Over the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: After Iran weaponized the waterway by making it too dangerous for businesses, experts say, the country is now looking to charge fees to vessels seeking to transit the vital water.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels anchored in the Strait of Hormuz, off the port city of Khasab on Oman’s northern Musandam Peninsula last month.

item.title: Delcy Rodríguez, Venezuela’s President, Struggles to Uphold Trump’s Narrative of Success
item.description: President Trump says Venezuela, under U.S. oversight, has “never made the money” it is making now. But new oil revenue isn’t helping ordinary Venezuelans, and anger seems to be mounting.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:description: Delcy Rodríguez, the interim president of Venezuela, during a meeting with American officials in Caracas in April.

item.title: At World Cup Soccer Game, Palestinian Emblems Abound
item.description: Jordan’s first qualification for the World Cup also means a World Cup berth for Palestinians, millions of whom live in the country.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Carlos Avila Gonzalez/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Fans of Jordan during the World Cup game against Algeria in Santa Clara, Calif., on Monday.

item.title: Those British Strawberries Are Being Picked by Central Asian Workers
item.description: Ten years after Brexit, most seasonal workers in Britain are from countries such as Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan. Without them, agricultural chiefs say, many farms would fail.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Aigerim Turgunbaeva
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shukrat Djuraev at Homefield Farm, in Kent, England, this month.

item.title: Iran Team Leaves Locker Room Note: The ‘Spirit of Iran Remains Alive and Steadfast’
item.description: After fighting Belgium to a draw for one of Iran’s best World Cup results, the Iranians had a note for their fans and the world.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iran national team has been allowed into the United States only for about 48 hours to play its games and then must leave the country.
